coredata.dc:title: Adsorption properties of chloropicrin on pristine and borazine-doped nanographenes: A theoretical study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Hosseinian, Akram
Vessally, Esmail
Babazadeh, Mirzaagha
Edjlali, Ladan
Es’haghi, Moosa
coredata.dc:description: Recently, nanographenes were introduced as definite segments of graphene where their end atoms are saturated with hydrogens. In this study, we explored the reactivity and electronic sensitivity of a hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) and newly synthesized borazine-like ring-doped nanographenes (BNG) to the chloropicrin molecule based on density functional theory. We found that chloropicrin is preferentially adsorbed via its N atoms on both HBC and BNG nanographenes. The electronic properties of HBC were predicted to be sensitive to chloropicrin but the adsorption capacity is low because of the small adsorption energy (−7.1 kcal/mol). However, chloropicrin is adsorbed somewhat more strongly on BNG, with an adsorption energy of about −29.9 kcal/mol. After the adsorption of chloropicrin, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of BNG stabilizes and the highest occupied molecular orbital-LUMO gap is decreased by about 85.9%. Thus, BNG converts from a semiconductor into a semimetal with a higher electrical conductivity. The change in electrical conductivity can generate an electrical signal, which is helpful for detecting chloropicrin. In addition, we predicted a short recovery time of 14.6 s at 350 K for this sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Density functional theory
Electronic structure
Nanographene
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction between chloropicrin and pristine HBC
Chloropicrin interaction with the pristine BNG

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structure of HBC nanographene and the DOS plot as well as its complexes with chloropicrin molecule. Distances are in Å. Yellow and red lines indicate unoccupied and occupied states, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Partial DOS for the C
O complex (Table 1), with its HOMO and LUMO profiles. Violet and red lines indicate chloropicrin and nanographene, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Optimized structure of BNG nanographene as well as the DOS plot and MEP surface. Color range in au: blue, more positive than 0.015; green, between 0.015 and 0; yellow, between 0 and −0.015; red, more negative than −0.015. In the DOS plot, the blue and green lines indicate unoccupied and occupied states, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Most stable complexes of chloropicrin/BNG and their DOS plots. Distances are in Å. In the DOS plot, the blue and green lines indicate unoccupied and occupied states, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Partial DOS for the B
O complex (Table 1), with its HOMO and LUMO profiles. Blue and red lines indicate chloropicrin and nanographene, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Energies of the HOMO, LUMO, and HOMO-LUMO gap (Eg) in eV, as well as the change in Eg after the adsorption of chloropicrin on the nanographenes. The adsorption energy (Ead) is in kcal/mol. %ΔEg indicates the percentage change in Eg after the adsorption of chloropicrin.

head.ce:title: Adsorption properties of chloropicrin on pristine and borazine-doped nanographenes: A theoretical study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering Science, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, P.O. Box 11365-4563, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, P.O. Box 19395-3697, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, nanographenes were introduced as definite segments of graphene where their end atoms are saturated with hydrogens. In this study, we explored the reactivity and electronic sensitivity of a hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) and newly synthesized borazine-like ring-doped nanographenes (BNG) to the chloropicrin molecule based on density functional theory. We found that chloropicrin is preferentially adsorbed via its N atoms on both HBC and BNG nanographenes. The electronic properties of HBC were predicted to be sensitive to chloropicrin but the adsorption capacity is low because of the small adsorption energy (−7.1 kcal/mol). However, chloropicrin is adsorbed somewhat more strongly on BNG, with an adsorption energy of about −29.9 kcal/mol. After the adsorption of chloropicrin, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of BNG stabilizes and the highest occupied molecular orbital-LUMO gap is decreased by about 85.9%. Thus, BNG converts from a semiconductor into a semimetal with a higher electrical conductivity. The change in electrical conductivity can generate an electrical signal, which is helpful for detecting chloropicrin. In addition, we predicted a short recovery time of 14.6 s at 350 K for this sensor.

ce:section.ce:para: Detecting chemical agents is vital for protecting workers in certain areas such as soldiers in the field, chemists in production plants, farm workers in the field, and hospital workers. Fumigants are an important class of possibly toxic chemical agents, which are used as pesticides in the form of gases that are heavier than air, where they can spread to all surfaces and areas [1]. Exposure to fumigants may have several effects on health, ranging from simple skin and eye irritation to more severe effects on the nervous system, while they can also mimic hormones to cause reproductive problems as well as cancer [2]. Chloropicrin, Cl3CNO2, is a fumigant that is used a powerful pesticide in agriculture to sterilize the soil [3]. It is also used as a chemical warfare agent [4]. Chloropicrin oxidizes the iron in hemoglobin, which leads to a condition known as methemoglobinemia where there is a high level of methemoglobin in the blood [5]. Thus, finding a sensitive, reliable, portable, rapidly responsive, and simple sensor for chloropicrin is very important.

Since the advent of nanotechnology, sensor development has accelerated because of the unique electronic sensitivity, high adsorption capacity, and high surface/volume ratio of nanostructures [6–15]. Thus, many nanostructure-based sensors have been introduced for different chemicals by experimental and theoretical researchers [16–25]. Graphene is one of the most important nanosheets and it has been used frequently as a chemical sensor [26–29]. In addition to infinite graphene, many studies have focused on zero-dimensional nanographenes (graphene molecules), which are finite parts of graphene where the end atoms are saturated with hydrogens [30–32]. The electronic structures of nanographenes differ from those of graphene because of their geometric confinement [33]. Nanographenes have high charge carrier mobility, high chemical and thermal stability, and good film-forming properties, which make them appropriate for use in organic field effect transistors, organic photovoltaics, organic electronics, organic light-emitting diodes, and transistors [34,35].

The interactions between different chemicals with zero-, one-, and two-dimensional carbon systems have been investigated frequently. Previous studies have indicated that alkali-adsorbed graphene nanoribbons exhibit feature-rich electronic and magnetic properties [36]. According to first-principles calculations, there are only a few adatom-dominated conduction bands, whereas the other conduction and valence bands are caused by carbon atoms. The feature-rich electronic and magnetic properties of fluorine-doped graphene nanoribbons have been investigated based on first-principles calculations [37]. Fluorine adatoms can generate p-type metals or concentration- and distribution-dependent semiconductors depending on whether π bonding is suppressed severely by top-site chemical bonding. High surface area nanographenes were employed for the adsorption and removal of p-nitroaniline from a model solution and wastewater sample [38].

Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC, C42H18) is an example of a popular nanographene that has received much attention because of its convenient preparation methods [39,40]. In addition, different HBC nanographenes have been synthesized where their central ring is doped with impurity atoms [41]. For example, in 2015, Krieng et al. [42] reported the synthesis of a HBC with a central borazine core (BNG). The structure of BNG is isotypic to that of the parent HBC. They suggested that this fascinating newly synthesized nanographene may have various applications such as in hydrogen storage. Furthermore, BNG may be used in NO2 sensor devices [43]. In the present study, we investigated the potential application of HBC and BNG structures for detecting chloropicrin gas based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

Density of states (DOS), molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analyses, and energy predictions were performed at the B3LYP level [44] of theory with the 6-31G (d) basis set as implemented in the GAMESS suite of programs [45]. The B3LYP functional is augmented with an empirical dispersion term [46] (B3LYP-D) to improve its trustworthiness when calculating noncovalent interactions. The GaussSum program was used to draw DOS plots [47]. The adsorption energy (Ead) was calculated as follows:
(1)
E
ad
= E
(
chloropicrin
/
HBC
)
− E
( HBC )
− E
( chloropicrin )
+ E
( BSSE )
,
where E (HBC) is the total energy of pristine or doped HBC, E (chloropicrin/HBC) is the total energy of the adsorbed chloropicrin molecule on the pristine or doped HBC surface, and E(BSSE) is the basis set superposition error (BSSE), which we obtained using the counterpoise method [48]. The energy calculated for BSSE in this study ranged from 0.2 to 3.1 kcal/mol. When we assessed the sensitivity of the sensor, the shift of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO)-lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy gap (Eg) was calculated by:
(2)
Δ
E g
=
[
(
E
g 2
−
E
g 1
)
/
E
g 1
]
∗
100 % ,
where Eg1 and Eg2 are the values of Eg for the bare nanostructure and the chloropicrin-adsorbed state, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between chloropicrin and pristine HBC
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized structure of the pristine HBC is shown in Fig. 1 , which demonstrates that it contains different CC bonds with bond lengths in the range of 1.38–1.45 Å, where these are in good agreement with the previously reported experimental results [49]. The sheet includes 48 C atoms and 18 H atoms. The predicted symmetry is D 6h with a small imaginary vibrational frequency (∼i2 cm−1), which is negligible. Based on the DOS plot in Fig. 1, the HOMO and LUMO energies are about −5.24 and −1.65 eV (Table 1 ), respectively. Thus, Eg is about 3.59 eV, which is very significant compared with the very low gap of graphene.

In order to investigate the adsorption of chloropicrin on the HBC sheet, it was placed on the sheet from its two heads, i.e., the Cl and N heads. After structural optimization, the two local minima obtained are shown in Fig. 1. Based on the adsorption energies obtained, the most stable complex (C
O) is that where chloropicrin is adsorbed from its oxygen atom on a C atom of the HBC. The adsorption energy for this process is about −7.1 kcal/mol and the interaction distance is about 2.99 Å. In the C
Cl complex, the molecule is relaxed on HBC via its Cl atoms, as shown in Fig. 1. The adsorption energy for this process is about −0.2 kcal/mol and the interaction distance is about 3.81 Å, which indicates that chloropicrin prefers to be adsorbed on HBC via its oxygen atoms rather than the chlorine atoms.

Our main aim was to predict the sensitivity of the Eg value of the studied sheets to the chloropicrin molecule. Many studies have indicated that Eg is related to the electrical conductivity according to Eq. (3), and it can be employed as a suitable index for a sensor's response to a chemical [50]:
(3)
σ =
AT
3 / 2
exp
(
−
E g
/
2 kT
)
,
where k is the Boltzmann constant and A (electrons/m3/K3/2) is a constant. Previous studies have shown that there is good agreement between the results obtained using this technique and the experimental results [51–66]. The results in Table 1 show that after the adsorption of chloropicrin via the two most probable configurations, the change in the LUMO level of HBC is significant. In both configurations, the LUMO level stabilizes considerably and shifts to lower energies. This can be explained by the fact that both heads of chloropicrin are nucleophilic and they tend to interact with the electron-deficient LUMO of the HBC. For example, in the most stable complex (C
O), the LUMO level shifts from −1.38 eV (in the bare BN-N nanocone) to −4.45 eV (Table 1). The LUMO profile of the C
O complex (Fig. 2 ) shows that its shape is changed significantly by shifting from the surface of HBC on the chloropicrin molecule, which is in good agreement with the large change in the energy. However, the HOMO is still on the HBC sheet and in agreement, there is a negligible energy change from −5.24 to −5.18 eV.

The partial DOS plot (Fig. 2) for the C
O complex shows that a new state appears via Eg, which comes mostly from the chloropicrin molecule. This is in good agreement with the LUMO profile (Fig. 2). As a result, the stabilization of the LUMO level implicitly decreases Eg for the HBC sheet. Eg decreases from 3.59 eV in the bare HBC sheet to 2.12 eV in the C
O complex, thereby indicating a decrease of about 41.1%. Therefore, the HBC becomes more conductive. Eq. (3) states that a decrease in Eg exponentially increases the population of conduction electrons in HBC, thereby increasing the electrical conductivity, which can be converted into an electrical signal. This signal is connected to the presence of chemicals in the environment. Thus, the presence of the chloropicrin molecule can be detected by HBC via the creation of noise if it is situated in an electrical circuit. However, although HBC is predicted to be electronically sensitive to chloropicrin, it is hindered by a weak interaction with a small adsorption energy, which decreases the gas adsorption capacity of HBC. For comparison, we studied the alternative BNG nanographene with polar BN bonds, which we consider may be more reactive toward polar molecules such as chloropicrin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chloropicrin interaction with the pristine BNG
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized structure of BNG is shown in Fig. 3 , which includes 42 C, 3 N, 3 B, and 18 H atoms. The BC and NC bonds are predicted to be about 1.51 and 1.42 Å, respectively. The diameter of the BN ring (∼2.89 Å) is somewhat larger than that in the pristine HBC (∼2.84 Å) due to the larger BN bonds (∼1.45 Å) compared with the CC bonds (∼1.42 Å). The MEP in Fig. 3 also shows that the surfaces of the N atoms have negative potential, whereas those of the neighboring C and B atoms are positive, which makes them appropriate sites for the adsorption of nucleophile agents such as chloropicrin. According to the DOS plot in Fig. 3 and the results in Table 1, the HOMO and LUMO energies of BNG nanographene are about −5.48 and −1.44 eV, respectively. Therefore, Eg is about 4.05, which is somewhat larger than that for HBC.

In order to reach the most stable chloropicrin/BNG complexes, the initial structures where the molecule is adsorbed from its O or Cl head on the B atoms of the BNG were subjected to structural optimization. Finally, two local minima were predicted as shown in Fig. 4 . Table 1 indicates that the total adsorption energy for the Cl head is about 0.3 kcal/mol (complex B
Cl) and the interaction distance is about 3.60 Å, which indicates that the interaction between chloropicrin and the Cl head is weaker with BNG compared with HBC, possibly because the lone pairs of N atoms on the BN ring in BNG repel the high electron Cl atoms. The DOS plot in Fig. 4 demonstrates that despite the weak interaction, the electronic properties of BNG are affected significantly by chloropicrin adsorption, where a new state appears at −3.11 eV and Eg is decreased by about 39.7%.

Unlike the weak interaction from the Cl head, the chloropicrin molecule interacts fairly strongly with the B atoms on BNG via its O head, as shown in Fig. 4 (complex B
O). For the B
O complex, the adsorption energy is about −29.9 kcal/mol and the interaction distance is about 1.65 Å. This adsorption energy provides BNG with a high adsorption capacity. For comparison, the adsorption energy of NO2 on BNG was calculated as about −5.1 kcal/mol based on the B3LYP-D functional and 6-31G(d) basis set [43], which indicates that the CCl3 group makes NO2 more reactive by pumping electrons on this molecule into chloropicrin. In this complex, the LUMO level shifts significantly from −1.43 eV (in the bare BNG) to −4.42 eV (Table 1). This change can be explained by the fact that the chloropicrin molecule interacts mainly with the LUMO level of the BNG via the lone pairs of N atoms. The LUMO profile of the B
O complex (Fig. 5 ) shows that the shape of the LUMO changes considerably and it shifts from the BNG surface on the chloropicrin molecule, which is in good agreement with the large shift in energy. By contrast the HOMO level is located (Fig. 5) on both the BNG and chloropicrin, which is consistent with its small change in energy.

The partial DOS plot of the B
O complex shows that a new state appears via Eg of the BO complex at −4.42 eV. According to the partial DOS plot, the adsorbed chloropicrin mainly contributes to the creation of this new state, which is in agreement with the LUMO profile. Nevertheless, the stabilization of the LUMO significantly decreases Eg for the BNG sheet by about 85.9%. Eg is reduced from 4.05 eV in the bare BNG to 0.57 eV in the B
O complex. Therefore, BNG is converted from a semiconductor into a semi-metal material after the adsorption process. Thus, the presence of the chloropicrin molecule can be detected and converted into an electrical signal by BNG. In contrast to the pristine HBC, the BNG has a more appropriate adsorption energy and higher sensitivity to chloropicrin as a sensor. Compared with chloropicrin, the NO2 gas can moderately decrease Eg for BNG by about 67.6% [43], thereby indicating that the presence of the CCl3 group on the NO2 group of chloropicrin makes this molecule much more readily detected by BNG compared with NO2 gas.

An important factor that affects the performance of a sensor is its recovery time. The recovery of a sensor is assessed experimentally by heating it to higher temperatures or by exposure to ultraviolet (UV) light [67]. The recovery time of the BNG sheet after chloropicrin desorption can be determined according to the following transition theory:
(4)
τ =
υ
− 1
exp
(
−
E
ad
/
kT
)
,
where T is temperature, k is Boltzmann's constant (∼1.99 × 10−3 kcal/mol°K), and υ is the frequency of attempts. Based on this equation, a much longer recovery time is expected if the adsorption energy increases considerably (more negative). If we use an attempt frequency under vacuum with UV of ∼3 × 1017°s−1, then the recovery with BNG is about 14.6 s at 350 K. Thus, the BNG had a short recovery time.

In this study, we investigated the adsorption of the chloropicrin molecule on HBC and BNG nanographenes based on DFT calculations. The chloropicrin molecule is preferentially adsorbed on both nanographenes via its oxygen atoms. The interaction process on the HBC nanocone involves weak physisorption, which releases energy of about 7.1 kcal/mol. The electronic properties of HBC change in a sensitive manner due to the adsorption process but it is not a suitable sensor because of the weak interaction. By contrast, BNG adsorbs the chloropicrin molecule strongly to release energy of about 29.9 kcal/mol. After the adsorption of chloropicrin, the LUMO level of BNG nanographene is stabilized greatly so Eg decreases from 4.05 to 0.57 eV. Consequently, BNG converts from a semiconductor into a semimetal and the electrical conductivity increases. This change in the electrical conductivity can produce an electrical signal, which is useful for sensing chloropicrin. We predicted a short recovery time of 14.6 s for this sensor at 350 K.
